title: Make command-line options cross-platform

comments.body:
Be careful that you don't code things such as `machine`, depending on the OS. We had a **lot** of code break, when people started running Docker Desktop also on Linux (and the code was assuming a local Docker Engine). It would be better to have a separate boolean, and then have it default to `true`  on Windows/macOS and to `false` when running on Linux ?

Podman is extreme, since you can't even run the same client program without a special `--remote` parameter or it will run the "server" (well, Engine) locally. But you don't want to embed these things deep in your logic, and then Podman Desktop starts supporting Linux and people start preferring that for some odd reason instead of just running Engine natively...

> `podman --fail-with "Skipping initialization (not needed)." machine init`
Well podman machine works on linux so I would say it should not skip it.

I don't think this is something podman should support, option parsing is already complicated enough I don't want to add more complexity when there is no real benefit for podman.

Podman works differently if it is remote or not, trying to hide this fact makes IMO things worse. Users must be aware that they are using the remote client when reading the docs. There are a lot of commands/options that only work in local mode.

> Users must be aware that they are using the remote client when reading the docs.

Yeah, wouldn't want to do crazy things like renaming `podman-remote` to `podman` 😉

>  Well podman machine works on linux so I would say it should not skip it.

On Arch Linux, it produces an error message:

``` 
$ pacman -S podman
resolving dependencies...
looking for conflicting packages...
... snip ...
:: Proceed with installation? [Y/n] Y
... snip ...
$ podman machine init
Error: exec: "qemu-system-x86_64": executable file not found in $PATH
$ podman machine start
Error: podman-machine-default: VM does not exist
```

What I'd like to do is have a simple installation flow for the wizard that captures all operating systems. Users of my application don't care about containers or virtualization, nor do they want to know the difference between remote and non-remote podman instances. They want high-quality PDFs. Unfortunately, ConTeXt has a [complex setup](https://github.com/DaveJarvis/keenwrite/blob/main/container/Containerfile) that I'm trying to spare users from tackling themselves, which is the impetus for using podman.

Here are some (incomplete) screenshots to give you a better idea. The first panel in the wizard tells what's happening:

![install-01](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2131950/208476711-358b3140-df1b-4f94-9f37-82702b0b18d5.png)

The wizard branches depending on platform. Here's Windows (incomplete):

![install-02](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2131950/208476716-2fa9313b-3c4d-4ef1-ac3e-53c151a60b66.png)

Here's *nix (mostly complete):

![install-linux](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2131950/208581517-bf9090ef-2f85-465d-9d7c-ec06a1c4e05d.png)

At this point, it would be nice if the wizard could converge back to a single flow line. 

![install-03](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2131950/208476720-59a7d699-4847-47b6-80ad-44624b754819.png)

On Windows and MacOS, running `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` works great. On Arch Linux, users would see the following message:

![install-04](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2131950/208589052-2ef57992-f57c-4ef9-bc18-77f41c895719.png)

That's why I was wondering whether there's anything we can do produce a different message. Users of my application's typesetting system's installation wizard shouldn't see an "error" initializing because it's misleading (from their perspective, anyway). I could suppress this flow for Linux, obviously, but my preference would be `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` work cross-platform without issuing any errors (unless it's an actual error that would affect the ability to import an image on Linux). Alternatively, if there was a way those commands could be run by default (e.g., during installation or when loading an image), that would be ideal.

We can certainly always improve errors, that just requires us to know which ones.

Your problem here is just that on arch the required qemu dependencies are not installed by default. I don't even know about the other distros like fedora and debian at the moment. It is perfectly fine to not install these dependencies since podman works natively (without an extra VM) on linux. It is really up to you or better your users to decide how they want to use podman.

> It is really up to you or better your users to decide how they want to use podman.

My users are writers, authors, bloggers, and scientists. They really and truly don't care about podman, virtual machines, containers, rootful/rootless environments, mount points, etc. They care about generating PDFs. Imagine Microsoft Word requiring its users to know how they should use podman before they can generate a PDF.

Pie-in-the-sky part two would be downloading and running podman standalone without going through an installer. This isn't possible for two reasons: (1) the installer has a version number, which prevents linking to the latest version (e.g., https://github.com/containers/podman/releases/latest/download/podman.exe); and (2) there's an installer GUI. Barring those issues, I could hide interacting with podman and ConTeXt altogether, simply asking for permission to download/install podman/ConTeXt, instead of having users jump through all these hoops, wizards, and other technical minutiae that are barriers to generating PDFs. However, a standalone headless version is an entirely different issue, so let's not get sidetracked. I only mention it to give you a flavour of what I'm trying to accomplish for the end users.

> We can certainly always improve errors, that just requires us to know which ones.

```
$ podman machine init
Error: exec: "qemu-system-x86_64": executable file not found in $PATH
$ podman machine start
Error: podman-machine-default: VM does not exist
```

Can we make those errors not be errors on Linux? Ideally, I'd like to control the message and hide the technical details (my users don't necessarily know about "qemu" or "podman-machine-default" or possibly even a "VM"---all those words are technobabble). For example, on Linux, I'd replace those error messages with the following phrases:

```
$ podman machine init
[INFO] Initialization is not required (skipping).
$ podman machine start
[INFO] Starting the virtual machine is not required (skipping).
```

Another idea would be to have a "log-level" argument that can specify a filter based on logging level:

```
$ podman --log-level error machine init
$ podman --log-level error machine start
```

That would only show any error messages that would prevent podman from loading or running containers. If the dependency on qemu isn't required, does that imply the "error" message has been miscategorized? Perhaps it should be `INFO` or `WARN` or `DEBUG` instead of `ERROR`? At that point, could the message be suppressed because it no longer falls into the `ERROR` category?

Another possibility would be to suppress messages if running on a particular platform:

```
$ podman --quiet linux machine init
$ podman --quiet linux machine start
```

The `--quiet linux` arg would be ignored if running on MacOS/Windows. On Linux, it'd only let "real errors" through. Meaning, there'd still need to be a way to qualify fundamental errors (i.e., "container won't operate without this") from other classes of issues.

I suspect, though, that we should probably just close this ticket and I'll change my wizard to not run those commands on Linux. (I don't really like this approach because it tightly couples knowledge of podman's inner workings on a particular operating system into my code.)
